data.title: Avis Budget Group: A Broad Q4 Miss, A Big Impairment, And A Familiar Outcome
data.description:
About 18 months ago, I rated Avis Budget Group, Inc. (CAR) as a Hold. Since then, the stock has been up a little over 10% vs. the 28% in the S&P 500 (SP500).

Back then, I was disappointed by the lack of insider buying activity, coupled with high operating expenses, vehicle depreciation, and interest expenses.

Today, the story is not materially different.

Q4 2025 was a broad miss, with Q4 and FY 2025 revenue missing the Street's consensus. However, the big shock was related to adjusted EBITDA, after the company missed management’s original FY25 target of “at least $1B” (which was later cut to $900M–$1.0B in Q2 2025).

CEO Brian Choi didn’t dress this underperformance up during the earnings call, stating:

“We fell significantly short of guidance… That’s unacceptable.”

As for the rationale, management points to the 43-day government shutdown and the cascading hit from FAA disruptions and TSA delays. On top of that, the company was hit with a $518M impairment in Q4 tied to the accelerating rotation of certain U.S. EV rental vehicles.

On the positive side, the International segment returned to profitability, and the $518M EV impairment could reduce depreciation pressure later this year. Is this enough to turn around the sentiment?

I argue the answer is no, as the pessimism has returned to this stock after the short-lived rally last year. Overall, I prefer to stay on the sidelines and reaffirm my hold rating on Avis.

Q4 2025: A Broad Miss On Results and Guidance

Q4 revenue came in at $2.66B, below the Street's $2.75B estimate. On a full-year basis, revenue was $11.65B, missing the consensus for the third consecutive year.

Revenue wasn't the metric that spooked investors.

That award goes to adjusted EBITDA, with the company reporting $748M for the full year vs. management's guidance in Q1 2025 of at least $1B for the year (which was downgraded to $900M–$1.0B in Q2). Since the downgrade in Q2, the stock dropped by 50% after a brief rally following Trump's reciprocal tariff pause in April last year.

On top of that, Q4 adjusted EBITDA was near zero vs. management's $157M guidance in Q3. Here are the words from CEO Brian Choi on missing the Q4 expectations:

I've said before that delivering on quarterly results is foundational, and when operational performance speaks for itself, we earn the right to focus on the bigger picture. This quarter, we didn't earn that right. We fell significantly short of guidance. That's unacceptable, and I have no excuses to offer.

The miss was largely attributed to the Americas segment.

In the October guidance, management expected the Americas rental days to grow by 3% in Q4 2025. Well, as you can see above, the actual rental days for the quarter were up by just 1%.

It seems that the government shutdown played a major role in the miss, both from government-related rental car demand and from the FAA flight reductions:

So while government travel immediately declined sharply following the shutdown, overall commercial demand initially held up. That changed abruptly in November.

FAA flight reductions, air traffic control disruptions, and extended TSA wait times materially reduced discretionary travel as it increased both uncertainty and inconvenience. Commercial rental days went from mildly down in October to down 11% in November. December stabilized, but by then, the damage to the quarter was done.

So, it seems that the demand pressure in the U.S. was driven by a macro headwind (i.e., the 43-day U.S. government shutdown) and not by a company-specific issue.

For this reason, I recommend keeping a close eye on Hertz's earnings next week. If they disappoint as well, that would be great news for Avis, as it would confirm the exogenous nature of the pressure in Q4. As a side note, Hertz is down 10% today in anticipation of poor Q4 results due to the shutdown.

Back to Avis, about $60M of the Q4 adjusted EBITDA miss was attributed to higher gross depreciation and lower gains on sale. This makes sense, as the Manheim Rental Index price per vehicle fell nearly $1,000 from October to November, reducing the gains on vehicle sales.

On top of that, the Americas monthly net depreciation per unit (you will see this as DPU in the financial reports) came in at $338 in Q4, versus an October estimate of "slightly lower than $300," naturally adding to the costs.

On depreciation, the guidance was another factor that contributed to the selloff post-earnings. DPU in early 2026 was guided upwards, with Q1 DPU around $400. Here is the rationale from the Q&A session:

in terms of the volatility that we saw in the 2025, you'll see that our models, which we're forecasting when we sell these cars into the future, they're built off of future forecasts, primarily in Black Book and Moody's. Those forward-looking economic models assume the impact of tariffs to continue throughout the life of these vehicles. As we've seen in the fourth quarter, that isn't the case anymore, so we've adjusted our internal models accordingly as well. So what you're seeing in the fourth quarter is kind of a catch up to show that reality and then normalizes over time.

What we're seeing is, listen, for six months of that elevated period where we saw tariff impacts, had we known it would evaporate, we probably would have been a little more conservative in our depreciation assumptions. What we're doing right now is just rectifying that in the first quarter to make sure that we reset to where we need to be.

The Upside

The headwinds seem to be mainly concentrated in the Americas region. As seen in the growth chart below, the International segment executed a turnaround in 2025.

Now, the international segment accounted for only 23% of total revenue in Q4, so I wouldn't pop a bottle of champagne just yet. That said, a quick look at the appendix of the Q4 earnings release shows a return to profitability in the international segment, with $21M in adjusted EBITDA vs. a loss of $11M last year.

It seems that the company has a certain level of pricing power outside of the U.S., as the revenue per car in this segment per day went up by 5% YOY to $56.76 in Q4.

On depreciation, after the spike in DPU in Q1, management projected a return to the low $300s later this year. Given management's track record of failing their own guidance, I remain skeptical.

Now, on the $518M impairment tied to the accelerating rotation of certain U.S. EV rental vehicles, I feel quite optimistic that this impairment was finally taken. I think this could be a tailwind for lower DPU in the back half of the year, given the higher depreciation costs of EVs (side note: that's why I proudly drive a 2004 TF 160).

Overall, management guided a FY26 adjusted EBITDA range between $800M–$1B. Compared to the $748M in FY25, that's an improvement. However, once again, I remain skeptical of this guidance.

Looking Ahead

I am not convinced to upgrade Avis to a Buy yet. From a technical perspective, I don't see any solid support until the mid-$50s, which represents another 40%+ drawdown.

While I agree with the bulls that the pressures in Q4 were mainly driven by macroeconomic events (mainly the government shutdown and the lower Manheim Rental Index price), the Americas segment accounts for 77% of total revenue. Therefore, the macro matters for the bull case. Right now, it is nearly impossible to gauge what the macro will look like this year.

On the positive side, lower interest rates should help from a debt refinancing perspective, which could lower interest payments, starting from the second half of the year. So far, Q4 has seen an increase in both vehicle interest expenses and corporate debt interest expenses.

However, the K-shaped economy may continue to pressure lower-end consumers, which is likely to further impact the Manheim Rental Index price index. If this happens again, similarly to Q4 last year, we may see another impact on adjusted EBITDA, and, after the FY downgrade in Q2 2025, we all know what that means for the share price.

Overall, I prefer to stay on the sidelines and monitor Hertz's (HTZ) Q4 earnings next week (pre-market February 26th) to confirm that the pressures are broad-based and not concentrated on Avis.
